data.title: Peabody Energy plunges after slashing Centurion mine Q1 sales outlook
data.description:
Peabody Energy (BTU) sank 9.6% in Monday's trading after saying it expects Q1 sales volume from its Centurion mine in Australia will come in below prior expectations, anticipating ~250K tons compared with prior forecasts of ~700K tons, based on greater-than-expected challenges in commissioning of the mines.

Despite the revised forecast, Peabody (BTU) said its FY 2026 metallurgical coal volume targets remain unchanged at 10.3M-11.3M tons.

Jefferies analysts maintained a Buy rating on Peabody (BTU) shares, citing high seaborne thermal coal prices as a potential offset to the impact of the shipment delay.

"High seaborne thermal coal prices will help offset the impact of this ramp-up delay, but this is still likely to be an overhang on BTU shares until it is fully resolved," Jefferies analyst Christopher LaFemina said in a note, adding that a successful ramp of Centurion is a "key component of the Peabody investment case."

"While today's news may be disappointing, we still believe Centurion will be a driver of substantial free cash flow and earnings growth for Peabody over the medium/longer term," LaFemina wrote.

Coal companies that produce thermal coal, which is used for power generation, have been gaining recently as investors reprice demand for coal as a heating source because prices for oil and natural gas are rising in response to the Middle East war; Newcastle coal futures settled Monday at $144.25/ton, its best close since November 2024 and up 23% in March, according to Dow Jones.

data.title: Sangamo targets summer 2026 for Fabry BLA submission while advancing neurology pipeline and partner talks
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sangamo Therapeutics (SGMO) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Alexander Macrae stated that "Sangamo continued to make significant pipeline progress in 2025 and into the first quarter of '26. Set against a backdrop of regulatory and market uncertainty and with limited cash resources." He highlighted positive top line results from the registrational STAAR study in Fabry disease, with eGFR slope at 52 weeks, and confirmed that the FDA reiterated this may serve as the primary basis for accelerated approval.
  • Macrae reported that the rolling submission of a BLA to the FDA for ST-920, Sangamo's first wholly owned BLA, is in progress. Sangamo has also transitioned to a clinical stage neurology company, activating six clinical sites in the Phase I/II STAND study for chronic neuropathic pain.
  • Macrae announced a third neurology capsid license agreement, this time with Eli Lilly, to deliver genomic medicines for up to five CNS disease targets. The company has raised over $130 million since the start of 2025 through nondilutive license fees, milestone payments, and equity financing.
  • Nathalie Dubois-Stringfellow, Chief Development Officer, stated, "We have now submitted both the nonclinical and the clinical modules to the FDA. In addition, the antibody assay companion diagnostic...has been submitted to and accepted by the FDA Center for Devices and Radiological Health." She added that the next milestone is completion of the chemistry, manufacturing, and controls (CMC) module, with manufacturing and testing of process validation lots completed and the first commercial lot manufactured.

Outlook

  • Macrae indicated that Sangamo anticipates "completing submission of the BLA as early as this summer, subject to our ability to secure adequate additional funding."
  • The company continues to seek a commercial partner for Fabry, which remains the top priority, with ongoing discussions involving multiple potential partners. Macrae described the process as complex and requiring extensive due diligence, stating, "Securing a commercial partner for Fabry remains our #1 focus, and we continue to engage in Fabry business development discussions with multiple potential partners."

Financial Results

  • Sangamo reported raising over $130 million in funding since the start of 2025 through nondilutive license fees, milestone payments, and equity financing, as stated by Macrae.
  • The company did not disclose specific quarterly revenue, EPS, or cash flow figures in the transcript.

Q&A

  • James Stamos, Jefferies LLC: "Can you provide more color on the revised timing for the Fabry BLA submission? What PPQ and other CMC-related activities are the primary gating factors here? ...can you comment on the status of the Fabry partnership discussions...?" Macrae responded that CMC is the critical path for filing and that timeline extensions are due to both agency requirements and prudent spending. Regarding partnerships, "most of the ones we spoke to last year have now gone. ...We are now speaking to multiple partners...these 3 are new to the discussions, and so we need to go through the process of due diligence, management presentations and then negotiations."
  • Luis Santos, H.C. Wainwright: "Have you had any additional interactions with the FDA recently or have any additional FDA interactions planned, specifically relating to the acceptance of the eGFR slope as a primary endpoint?" Macrae answered that the FDA reiterated in October the use of eGFR at one year for accelerated approval, and Sangamo does not plan further agency meetings unless there are regulatory changes.
  • Unknown Analyst: Asked about the timeline for the first in-human trial for STAC-BBB capsid and suitability of the capsid for different indications. Macrae explained that the choice of AAV9 for neuropathic pain was due to program initiation prior to STAC-BBB development and confirmed ongoing collaborations and positive relationships with partners utilizing STAC-BBB.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on clarity around regulatory timelines, partnership progress, and BLA submission, showing a slightly cautious to neutral tone with a keen interest in factors that may impact commercialization.
  • Management maintained a confident and factual tone, emphasizing progress and regulatory clarity while openly acknowledging challenges related to cash runway and partnership timing. Macrae stated, "we are following...very clear detailed pages of guidance on what was needed to complete the submission and maximize its chance of success," and "we will not be satisfied until we solve our long-term cash runway."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment remains consistent, focusing on regulatory and partnership milestones, while management continues to demonstrate steady confidence but remains realistic about financial constraints.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter features advancement in the BLA rolling submission, with both clinical and nonclinical modules submitted, compared to the previous quarter's preparation phase.
  • The number of active clinical sites for the STAND study increased from two to six.
  • The company raised over $130 million in new funding, while the previous quarter focused on extending the cash runway with smaller milestones such as a $6 million license fee.
  • Strategic focus on Fabry commercialization partnership and neurology pipeline remains consistent, though management noted that new partners are now engaged versus those from last year.
  • Management and analysts maintain a similar tone, but there is increasing emphasis on the urgency of securing partnerships and funding.

Risks and Concerns

  • Macrae acknowledged that "we will not be satisfied until we solve our long-term cash runway," highlighting ongoing financial constraints.
  • The CMC module remains a gating factor for the BLA submission, requiring detailed compliance with FDA guidance.
  • Partnership negotiations are complex and time-consuming, with turnover among interested parties and continued regulatory uncertainty influencing the pace of deals.

Final Takeaway

Sangamo Therapeutics is progressing toward completing the BLA submission for its Fabry disease gene therapy as early as this summer, having already submitted key modules and advanced manufacturing requirements. The neurology pipeline is expanding, with six active clinical sites and a new Fast Track designation for a lead asset. Management continues to emphasize the need for additional funding and a commercial partner for Fabry, with new negotiations underway. The company highlights its regulatory engagement and pipeline achievements while being candid about the complexities and challenges ahead in securing long-term financial stability and partnership agreements.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: RTX unit secures $125M USTRANSCOM contract for simulation tools
data.description:
• RTX BBN Technologies, a Raytheon (RTX) unit, secured a $125M contract to provide simulation and optimization tools for joint deployment and distribution analysis.
  • The performance period runs from April 1, 2026, to March 31, 2031.
  • Working capital funds for FY2026 will be used for individual task orders, with the U.S. Transportation Command as the contracting activity.

data.title: Intellinetics outlines sharper go-to-market focus and SaaS growth target amid margin gains and leadership transition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Intellinetics, Inc. (INLX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Alison Forsythe, President & CEO, highlighted her recent transition into the CEO role and her initial focus on getting close to the business, assessing operations, and identifying improvement opportunities. She stated, "What I see is a company with strong foundational assets and established SaaS customer base, differentiated technology and meaningful opportunities in vertical markets where secure document and workflow automation remain critical." Forsythe reported continued SaaS revenue growth and an important contractual win with the company's largest customer, noting that SaaS now represents an increasing share of the overall business.

  • Forsythe emphasized, "Our software platform is the core of the business and the primary driver of growth and long-term value creation." Strategic priorities were cited as improving execution, sharpening the go-to-market approach, and accelerating SaaS revenue, while aiming for greater consistency in the document management business.

  • Joseph Spain, CFO & COO, provided, "Total revenue for the quarter increased a net 1% to $4.3 million, with growth in SaaS revenues covering decreases in other revenue lines." Spain highlighted, "SaaS, including hosting revenue, increased 8.4% to $1.6 million for the quarter from $1.5 million for the same period last year." He also noted, "Gross margin increased 184 basis points to 66.6% for Q4 compared to 64.8% last year," attributing the improvement to a greater proportion of higher-margin SaaS revenue and price increases.

Outlook

  • Forsythe and Spain signaled a continued focus on SaaS expansion, with Spain stating, "Based on our current plans and assumptions and subject to risks and uncertainties we described in our filings and this call, we expect that we will grow SaaS revenues on a year-over-year basis for fiscal year 2026."

  • Management did not provide specific revenue or EPS guidance figures. The emphasis remains on SaaS revenue growth and improved execution, with a strategy centered on accelerating SaaS and bringing consistency to other business lines.

Financial Results

  • Q4 total revenue was $4.3 million, up 1% year-over-year, driven by SaaS growth offsetting declines in other areas. SaaS revenue increased 8.4% to $1.6 million. Software and maintenance services were down 11.2% year-over-year. Professional services revenue declined by 1.8%, totaling approximately $2.2 million, and remained below expected levels following the June 2025 contract renewal with the largest customer.

  • Consolidated gross margin for Q4 improved to 66.6% from 64.8%. Operating expenses rose 11.2% to $3.1 million, attributed to investments in sales, marketing, and infrastructure. Q4 net loss was $208,000, or ($0.05) per share, compared to a net loss of $54,000, or ($0.01) per share, in the prior year period. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $261,000, down from $531,000 last year.

  • For the full year, total revenue was $16.6 million, down 8% from $18 million. SaaS revenue grew 11.1%, while professional services revenue decreased 18.7%. Full year net loss was $1.873 million, or ($0.44) per share, versus $546,000, or ($0.13) per share, last year. Adjusted EBITDA for 2025 was $470,000 compared to $2.4 million for 2024.

  • As of December 31, 2025, cash was $2.5 million, accounts receivable net $1.2 million, total assets $17.3 million, and total liabilities $5.9 million. No debt remained on the balance sheet.

Q&A

  • NOT_APPLICABLE: No analyst questions or management responses were included in the Q4 2025 transcript.

Sentiment Analysis

  • NOT_APPLICABLE: No analyst questions present in the current transcript to assess analyst sentiment.

  • Management’s tone was focused on opportunity and improvement, with Forsythe stating, "Our focus now is execution, sharpening our go-to-market approach and accelerating SaaS growth while bringing greater consistency to our document management business."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management tone shifted from discussing pipeline building and product momentum to emphasizing operational execution and consistency. The prior quarter featured more detailed discussion of customer wins and product expansion, whereas the current quarter focused on internal improvements and future SaaS growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The CEO role transitioned from James DeSocio in Q3 2025 to Alison Forsythe in Q4 2025, with a new emphasis on execution and go-to-market strategy.

  • Q4 revenue increased slightly, but the overall annual revenue declined from the previous year. SaaS revenue growth remained consistent, while professional services continued to show weakness post-renewal of the largest customer contract.

  • Management shifted from describing sales pipeline and product launches in Q3 to focusing on execution, margin improvement, and operational consistency in Q4.

  • Operating expenses increased in Q4, reflecting higher investments in marketing and infrastructure, compared to reductions in share-based compensation and variable expenses in Q3.

  • Analyst tone cannot be assessed for Q4 due to the absence of Q&A; in Q3, analysts were engaged and focused on SaaS ramp and product cross-sell.

Risks and Concerns

  • Forsythe acknowledged opportunities to strengthen execution and improve consistency in the document management business, indicating execution risk.

  • Spain noted that professional services revenue "has not recovered to a level we expected following the June '25 renewal of our largest customer contract," highlighting ongoing variability in this segment.

  • Increased operating expenses tied to investments in sales, marketing, and engineering development may pressure margins until growth materializes.

  • Management flagged risks and uncertainties in their filings and call commentary, but specific mitigation strategies were not detailed in the transcript.

Final Takeaway

Intellinetics leadership transition brings renewed focus on operational execution and SaaS growth as the company works to improve margin profile and consistency in its document management business. Management remains optimistic about the company’s foundational assets and vertical market opportunities, prioritizing acceleration of SaaS revenue and sharpening its go-to-market strategy for long-term value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Spruce Power signals continued margin expansion and strategic refinancing while scaling Spruce Pro
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Spruce Power Holding Corporation (SPRU) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Christopher Hayes, President, CEO & Chairman, highlighted, “2025 was a breakout year for Spruce and our fourth quarter capped it with exceptional momentum across the business.” He emphasized strong revenue growth, expanded margins, improved efficiency, and enhanced scalability. Hayes reported that for Q4, revenue was approximately $24 million, up 19% year-over-year, and operating EBITDA exceeded $17 million. For the full year, revenue increased 36% compared to 2024, driven by both organic growth and the impact of the NJR acquisition. He noted, “Importantly, this growth was accompanied by substantial operating leverage,” with significant declines in O&M and SG&A expenses, reflecting ongoing cost optimization. The CEO also addressed a going concern disclosure, stating, “It is not reflective of our operating performance or lender engagement.” Hayes described the extension of the SP1 facility to create flexibility for a broader refinancing transaction, targeting alignment with the company’s scaled platform.

  • Hayes outlined three key growth drivers: acquiring installed residential solar portfolios, expanding programmatic partnerships with developers and originators, and scaling the capital-light Spruce Pro servicing platform.

  • Thomas Cimino, Chief Financial Officer, stated, “For the fourth quarter 2025, revenue totaled $24 million compared to $20.2 million in the fourth quarter 2024. The increase was again primarily attributable to the residential solar portfolio acquired from NJR...as well as higher solar renewable energy credit revenue.” Cimino further emphasized, “Total operating expense was $21.8 million for the quarter compared to $26.7 million in the year earlier period.” He highlighted ongoing cost improvements and detailed the extension of the SP1 facility, which now runs through January 30, 2027, and requires a term sheet by October 30, 2026.

Outlook

  • Management aims to build on second half momentum by continuing to reduce costs and improve the recurring run rate core expense profile while pursuing “modest disciplined growth.” There was no explicit revenue or EPS guidance issued for Q1 2026 or the full year, nor any analyst estimates provided for comparison. The company’s strategy remains focused on generating stable cash flow, acquiring new portfolios, expanding partnerships, and scaling Spruce Pro.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter 2025 revenue was $24 million, up from $20.2 million in Q4 2024. Operating EBITDA for the quarter was $17 million, compared to $10.8 million in the prior year period. Adjusted cash flow from operations for the quarter was $5.1 million, a turnaround from negative $4.1 million the prior year.

  • Core operating expenses (SG&A and O&M) were $14.9 million, with SG&A at $13 million and O&M at $1.9 million. Total operating expense was $21.8 million, down from $26.7 million year-over-year.

  • For the full year, operating EBITDA was $80.1 million, a 49% increase over 2024. Income from operations was $17.9 million, a significant improvement from negative $50.4 million in the previous year.

  • The company repaid $35.1 million in debt during 2025, closing the year with $93.1 million in cash. Total outstanding principal debt at year-end was $695.5 million with a blended interest rate of 6.1%.

Q&A

  • William Hamilton, Kestrel Merchant Partners: “I just wanted to see if I could get a little bit more color on the revenue buckets. How much was SREC during the quarter and the services revenue since those have been larger growth contributors?” Thomas Cimino responded, “The SREC revenue for the year was $21 million and the system, either leases or PPA revenue was $78 million...the SP4 revenue is consistent with every quarter...recorded below the line as interest income.”

  • Hamilton: “How would you characterize like sort of the pipeline of adding new business [to Spruce Pro]?” Christopher Hayes replied, “We have a robust pipeline that's made up of kind of what we call a few large whales and sort of some smaller opportunities. We didn't announce anything in the quarter, but we are hopeful there will be announcements in the near term and are very aggressive in that space.”

  • Hamilton: “On M&A...is it also now kind of tied to the debt consolidation deal that you're working on?” Hayes answered, “We do have a super active pipeline...We are underwriting a number of deals, whether we get to closing remains to be seen, but that is certainly the objective...there is not an interplay with [the SP1 extension] and either helping or hurting any strategic growth acquisitions that sort of operate independently.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on understanding the composition of revenue and the growth pipeline, with a neutral to slightly positive tone, expressing interest in operational performance and growth prospects.

  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone in prepared remarks, frequently using language such as “exceptional momentum,” “structural gains,” and “robust pipeline.” In the Q&A, management’s tone remained direct and optimistic, providing specific information and addressing each question thoroughly.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment remained constructive, with questions focused on revenue breakdown and growth initiatives. Management’s tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A continued to demonstrate confidence, though this quarter included a more detailed discussion of refinancing strategy and associated disclosures.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue for Q4 2025 was lower than Q3 2025, reflecting seasonality, but year-over-year growth remained strong. Operating EBITDA and profitability metrics improved year-over-year. Debt principal was reduced further in Q4.

  • Management’s strategic focus remained consistent, with continued emphasis on portfolio acquisitions, programmatic partnerships, and expansion of Spruce Pro. However, this quarter included a notable shift as management discussed the going concern disclosure and outlined a broader refinancing initiative.

  • Analyst questions remained focused on revenue breakdown and growth pipeline, similar to the prior quarter. Both management and analysts maintained a steady tone, with no significant increase in skepticism or concern.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management addressed the going concern disclosure, attributing it to accounting requirements related to the ongoing refinancing process and not to operational performance or lender engagement.

  • The company is actively managing refinancing and capital structure risk, having extended the SP1 facility and planning a comprehensive refinancing solution.

  • Seasonality in solar generation and customer payments continues to impact quarterly revenue and cash flows, but management emphasized the stability of underlying cash generation.

Final Takeaway

Spruce Power concluded 2025 with robust revenue growth, margin expansion, and improved cash generation, driven by disciplined cost controls and the integration of acquired portfolios. Management remains confident in the ongoing refinancing process and is focused on leveraging its scalable platform for further growth through portfolio acquisitions, expanded partnerships, and the scaling of Spruce Pro. The company’s strengthened operating performance, reduced leverage, and active growth pipeline support its outlook for continued momentum into 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: RTX wins $3.81B U.S. Navy F-135 engine production contract modification
data.description:
• RTX secures a $3.81B fixed-price incentive contract modification for the production of F135 engines for F-35 jets for the Joint Strike Fighter program.
  • The work is expected to be completed by March 2028.
  • Funding includes FY24 Navy $0.22B; FY24 Air Force $0.21B; FY25 Navy $0.80B; FY25 Air Force $0.73B; F-35 partner funds $0.38B; and FMS funds $1.47B, with $0.43B expiring this fiscal year.
  • Naval Air Systems Command is the contracting activity.

data.title: Gold rides momentum higher as safe-haven demand picks up
data.description:
Gold and silver futures finished with firm gains Monday, as traders have stepped up safe-haven buying recently with the Middle East war continuing to rage, plus an assist from lower global government bond yields and higher crude oil prices.

Traders have been treating gold like a risky asset instead of a safe-haven parking spot for money, but that shift in sentiment appears to be easing as President Trump posts about making a deal with Iran, Thadeu Dos Santos of Infinox said in a note.

"Geopolitical risks remain elevated, supporting safe-haven demand, but the market continues to weigh this against a more restrictive U.S. rate outlook," Dos Santos said.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for April delivery closed up 0.7% to $4,256/oz, its third gain in the past four trading sessions, and front-month Comex April silver (XAGUSD:CUR) gained 1.1% to $70.324/oz, rising for the fourth time in five sessions.

Gold has fallen 13.5% so far ​in March, placing the metal on track for its worst monthly showing since 2008, as surging energy ‌prices have ⁠heightened inflation concerns and caused markets to reassess their interest rate expectations.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)
